icd_category: K70.30 — Alcoholic cirrhosis of liver without ascites
one_liner: A 55-year-old male with a 25-year history of heavy alcohol use presents with progressive abdominal distension, lower extremity edema, and fatigue over 3 months.

stage2.hpi_extension: The patient reports progressive abdominal swelling over the past 3 months with a 10 kg weight gain despite decreased appetite. He has noticed bilateral lower extremity swelling and easy fatigability. He reports dark urine and pale stools intermittently. He has had two episodes of confusion noted by his wife, with disorientation and sleep-wake reversal lasting 1-2 days each. He has noticed increased bruising with minimal trauma. He denies hematemesis, melena, or hematochezia. He has consumed 6-8 beers daily for 25 years and half a bottle of whiskey on weekends. He attempted to cut down 6 months ago but continued drinking.
stage2.pmh: Hypertension, gastritis, prior episode of this condition hepatitis 3 years ago
stage2.medications: Amlodipine 10 mg daily, omeprazole 20 mg daily, thiamine 100 mg daily
stage2.family_history: Father died of liver disease at age 60. Brother has alcohol use disorder.
stage2.social_history: Active heavy alcohol use (6-8 beers daily for 25 years, plus liquor on weekends). Former smoker (20 pack-years, quit 2 years ago). Unemployed, previously worked in construction. Divorced. Lives alone.
stage2.physical_exam: General: Chronically ill-appearing male with temporal wasting and muscle wasting. HEENT: Scleral icterus, parotid gland enlargement. Cardiovascular: Regular rate, no murmurs. Pulmonary: Decreased breath sounds at right base. Abdomen: Markedly distended with shifting dullness and fluid wave consistent with ascites, caput medusae visible on abdominal wall, liver edge not palpable, spleen palpable 3 cm below left costal margin. Skin: Jaundice, multiple spider angiomata on the chest and face, palmar erythema, Dupuytren contracture of the right hand. Extremities: 3+ bilateral pitting edema to the knees. Neuro: Alert, oriented to person and place but not date, asterixis present bilaterally.

lab_results.clinical_significance: Pancytopenia with macrocytic anemia from folate deficiency/alcohol toxicity; thrombocytopenia from splenic sequestration

lab_results.result: Na 128, K 3.2, Cr 1.3, BUN 22, Glucose 88, Albumin 2.4, Total protein 5.8
lab_results.clinical_significance: Hyponatremia (dilutional from ascites), hypokalemia, impaired synthetic function (low albumin)

lab_results.result: AST 98, ALT 52, ALP 145, Total bilirubin 4.8, Direct bilirubin 3.2
lab_results.clinical_significance: AST:ALT ratio >2:1 is classic for this condition liver disease; elevated bilirubin from impaired hepatic conjugation
Prolonged from impaired hepatic synthesis of clotting factors
Elevated ammonia consistent with hepatic encephalopathy
Markedly elevated, consistent with chronic alcohol use and cholestasis

lab_results.result: HBsAg negative, anti-HBs positive, anti-HBc negative
lab_results.clinical_significance: Immune from vaccination, no active or prior HBV infection

lab_results.result: SAAG 1.8, Total protein 1.5, WBC 120 (65% lymphocytes), culture negative
lab_results.clinical_significance: SAAG >1.1 confirms portal hypertension; low protein ascites typical of this condition; no SBP
Normal, no evidence of hepatocellular carcinoma

imaging_results.result: Liver is small and nodular with coarse heterogeneous echotexture. Large-volume ascites. Splenomegaly (16 cm). Patent portal vein with hepatofugal flow. No focal liver lesions. No biliary dilation.
imaging_results.clinical_significance: confirms the suspected diagnosis with portal hypertension (hepatofugal flow, splenomegaly, ascites). No HCC on initial screening.

imaging_results.result: Right-sided pleural effusion. No infiltrate or cardiomegaly.
imaging_results.clinical_significance: Hepatic hydrothorax from ascitic fluid transudation through diaphragmatic defects.

stage3.key_tests_rationale: LFTs with AST:ALT ratio >2:1 is characteristic of alcoholic liver disease. Albumin and INR assess hepatic synthetic function and determine Child-Pugh/MELD scores. CBC reveals thrombocytopenia from portal hypertension. Ultrasound with Doppler confirms cirrhotic morphology and evaluates portal hemodynamics.

stage5.final_clue: A 55-year-old heavy drinker with jaundice, ascites, splenomegaly, hepatic encephalopathy, and AST:ALT >2:1 with impaired synthetic function (low albumin, elevated INR). Ultrasound shows a nodular liver with portal hypertension and hepatofugal flow. SAAG >1.1 confirms portal hypertensive ascites. These findings confirm decompensated this condition this condition (Child-Pugh C).

debrief.classic_presentation: Alcoholic cirrhosis presents with signs of chronic liver disease (jaundice, spider angiomata, palmar erythema, gynecomastia, caput medusae) and portal hypertension (ascites, splenomegaly, varices). Decompensation is marked by ascites, variceal bleeding, hepatic encephalopathy, or jaundice. The AST:ALT ratio >2:1 is a hallmark of alcohol-related liver injury. Patients often have concurrent nutritional deficiencies.
debrief.pathophysiology: Chronic alcohol consumption causes hepatocyte injury through acetaldehyde (a toxic metabolite of ethanol via alcohol dehydrogenase), oxidative stress, and inflammation. Repeated injury activates hepatic stellate cells, leading to progressive fibrosis and nodular regeneration (cirrhosis). Increased intrahepatic resistance causes portal hypertension, leading to portosystemic shunting, splenomegaly, ascites, and varices. Impaired hepatic clearance of ammonia and other toxins causes hepatic encephalopathy. Loss of synthetic function results in hypoalbuminemia, coagulopathy, and hyperbilirubinemia.
debrief.treatment_overview: Alcohol cessation is the most critical intervention — refer to addiction medicine. Ascites management: sodium restriction (<2 g/day), diuretics (spironolactone 100 mg + furosemide 40 mg in 100:40 ratio), therapeutic paracentesis with albumin replacement for large-volume ascites. Hepatic encephalopathy: lactulose (titrate to 2-3 soft stools/day) and rifaximin 550 mg BID. Variceal screening: EGD for esophageal varices, prophylaxis with non-selective beta-blockers (nadolol or propranolol). SBP prophylaxis if ascitic fluid protein <1.5 g/dL. Calculate MELD score for transplant eligibility. Nutritional support, thiamine, and folate supplementation.
high_yield_pearls: AST:ALT ratio >2:1 is the hallmark of alcoholic liver disease — ALT requires pyridoxal phosphate (B6) as a cofactor, which is deficient in alcoholics.
MELD score (bilirubin, INR, creatinine) is used to prioritize liver transplant listing; MELD >15 generally warrants transplant evaluation.
SAAG (serum-ascites albumin gradient) >1.1 g/dL indicates portal hypertension with 97% accuracy.
Six months of documented alcohol abstinence is traditionally required for liver transplant listing, though some centers now offer early transplant for select patients.
SBP should be suspected in any cirrhotic patient with ascites who develops fever, abdominal pain, or encephalopathy — diagnose with ascitic fluid PMN count >250/mm3.
sources: 2018 AASLD Practice Guidance on Alcohol-Related Liver Disease
Singal AK et al., ACG Clinical Guideline: Alcoholic Liver Disease, American Journal of Gastroenterology 2018
European Association for the Study of the Liver (EASL) Guidelines on Decompensated Cirrhosis 2018
